sentences.text: Well, we-- I have to tell you first what happened during this disinfection.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In this, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what I say bath, or shower, or de-lousing station, everything that connected us with the past was lost.
We were stripped to our skin, and we were shaved from head to toe.
All our top hair, all our pubic hair, everything was shaved, down to the toes.
We had to undergo rectal examination because they suspected that we had some gold or jewelry hidden there.
We-- mind you, in October, the climate in Poland is like winter.
We shivering standing around there.
We were chased into this shower room, and cold water came out of the showers.
We didn't have any soap [INAUDIBLE].
And we were just washed down.
There was no towel to dry up, and we were chased out into the cold.
And standing naked, as we are, in assembly line, which is called the appel.
And many, many hundred of time in the future, we had to stand in this kind of appel.
It is an unbelievable, demeaning situation to be stark naked, because clothes somehow is a protection.
The physical assault was such that a lot of us just dropped and fell, and were-- they were just removed.
After hours of this ordeal, we finally were given filthy garb, which is the striped uniforms of the prisoners.
There were blood stains, and torn.
We had no socks, no underwear.
We got some rough, strange shoes.
And I may tell a special story about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Dr.
Solomon, who happened to have high military boots.
Boots were very much in demand by the German SS, and these boots would have been, of course, confiscated.
But somehow, he feverishly cut off the sleeves of the boots, so that there was only the shoe left.
And there-- he held on to the shoes for dear life.
And later on, we only realized why he did that.
When the SS saw what he had done, they beat him half to death because he ruined some boots that they could have used.
But somehow, he held onto his shoes and could use them, and we only learned much later why he was almost giving his life to hold on to those shoes.
But I come to this later.
We finally were given this uniform and clothes, and we were to marched back to our barracks.
The barracks were just plain misery.
There were, again, these three-tiered sleeping arrangements, with cubicles barely enough for four people.
We had to sleep 10 of them.
In order to sleep, we had to sleep on our sides, because we couldn't stretch out on our backs.
There was absolutely no food.
As we learned later, our blockalteste, or kapo, of course received our rations, if you want to call it, pieces of bread and some soup.
But he knew that our stay there will not be very long, and we are doomed anyway, so he sold our rations for himself, and we were left hungry.
And we--
Pardon?
No, this kapo-- all the kapos in-- in Birkenau were criminals, which were marked by a yellow-- by a red triangle.
We had yellow triangles.
There were political prisoners who had a green triangle.
They were all marks so you knew which one-- and the Jew, of course, had to have a-- a Jew-- a Juden on it in addition.
It was not a group.
Just this one guy, and he was quite a guy.
We would have probably dropped and died right there and then because of hunger and exposure, but by coincidence, a friend of ours, Mr.
Kurt Meyer, who was a piano-- a pianist, saw us.
And he, being a piano player, was in the high elite.

He entertained the SS and the German soldiers with his piano playing, and therefore, he had access to as much food as he wanted, and he brought two baskets full of bread and-- and saved our lives right there and then.
Like everything else, our life in-- in Auschwitz consisted of moping around.
We were not working yet, and of course, every two or three hours there was a whistle and assembly, and we were counted, and counted again, and recounted.
And while we were standing there, we got acquainted with a new wonderful thing called selection.

The selection took place by some kind of an SS officer who went with his entourage and picked out people out of our line up who seemed to not fit enough to do labor, or elderly, and send them to the other side.
And you know what happened to them.
At that time, there was the suspicion.
But of course, we did-- we somehow couldn't believe that this is the case.
We did believe that they did away with the hopelessly sick people, crippled people, and very old people.
They-- I-- we were quite sure, were eliminated one way or the other.
We didn't know about the technicality at that time yet.
But we somehow held on to the hope that our-- our people will be still alive, our families.
So there was one appel, or assembly, after the other, one selection after the other, the miserable nights, the freezing.

And one day, we were lined up to become tattooed on our forearm, and while we were standing in line, a-- a commander came and said, you, you, you, and you, and picked out a few of us, and we were given winter coats, which was already an enormous luxury because it was freezing.
And we were marched with the usual amenities, like whipping, and beatings, and shootings.
We were marched to the trail, and again, we were put in a railroad-- into a cattle train.
This time, we got a piece of bread on our trip, and so things were looking up.
And we were on the train two full days until we arrived at the place, at a railroad station with the name of Kaufering.
Six days.
That's all.
Well, Theresienstadt was a trenchant ghetto.
As we saw, even Birkenau was a trenchant ghetto.
Most of the people, I mean over 90%, were killed right there and then.
But the ones who were fit were destined to die in another way.
That is, through work and starvation.
So we arrived in Kaufering, which I later learned is one of the branches, or one of the slave labor camps, attached to another famous concentration camp with the name of Dachau.
And whenever we thought we have reached a low that a human being can go through, we were bitterly disappointed because we learned there is still much, much worse to come.
The stay in Kaufering was just one of continuous horror.
We were there to be worked to death, and there was no other purpose.
Our sleeping arrangements were the old barracks like in Birkenau.
We had appel after appel, no decent clothes.
We had, of course, absolute no sanitary-- we had a latrine, but there was no way of getting washed.
I didn't know what a toothbrush looks like for six months.
We had no underwear.
We had no change of clothes.
If one of the uniforms became very much torn, you got another one which was not much better.
But it was just a existing that nobody could ever assume was possible.
Kaufering was near Dachau, near Munich, in Germany, in Bavaria.
Yes.
In Bavaria-- Bavaria is one high plateau.
It's about 800 meters above the sea level.
That means the winters are very, very harsh.
There's a lot of snow and bitter, bitter cold.
The amount of freezing we did there was just unbelievable.
We had to get up at the crack of dawn.
We had to stand in endless assemblies and appels because we were counted, and again counted, and again counted.
And then we were put it into work details.
I, with my colleagues, were in a detail which is called the [NON-ENGLISH].
And what we had to do, first we had to march about an hour to our working place.
We always marched on back roads.
We never saw any German inhabitants, and they were not allowed to see us.
And it was still dark and night, it was so early in the morning.
Once we arrived there, our labor started.
It was always the heaviest of heavy physical labor.
Long forgotten was that I was a doctor.
I was just one of the slave laborers.
Well, first we were on a detail, we had to load these little hand-driven rail cars with heavy stones, with gravel.
And we were not used to this kind of work, so there were a lot of injuries.
But that was-- nobody could give in to any bleeding or injury because we knew what would happen if you would give up or fall.
So we trundled away.
Whenever you had to go to the latrine, you had to ask permission, military fashion.
We who were in an army, we had it fairly easy.
But the other guys who had to learn all this rigmarole of standing in attention, and-- and loud, and yell loud out what you wanted.
When you went to the latrine, if you lingered more than half a minute, you were hit over the head from the outside.
And so there was no way of resting there.
And the-- the days were endless.
You had to count every minute and every hour, and the clock didn't seem to go.
And there was this bitter cold, which was alleviated by the labor, because you-- you got quite warm through the labor, but there was always this gnawing, incredible hunger pain and the empty stomach.
And you saw how you became thinner and thinner.
And when the work was over, again we had to go into formation, march home, and again, we had to stand in appel.
We were counted over and over again.
And finally, we were given this kind of a dirty water that they called soup.
And it was always a piece of luck when you were on line and the kettle came to the bottom because then you had the luck to get maybe a piece of potato or a leaf of cabbage in it.
Otherwise, you just had this hot, dirty water.
But at least it was something to get into your stomach.
And we gulped it down, and that was the end of the day.
Then we crept into our bunks and slept like logs, sleeping always on the side because there was no room to stretch out.
The nights were bitter cold.
You-- if you had to go out, you had to go outside, and being hungry, you had a very full bladder all the time because water was there, and your drank an awful lot of water.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What were the nights life?
We-- we were exhausted from this labor, so we slept.
Yeah.
Seven days a week.
The-- the life there was horrible.
We had our enemies counted out.
We had, of course, the SS people, who were enemy number one.
They couldn't help it, beating-- whatever.
Whenever they could, they hit somebody over the head or hit over the back.
And this hurt, this hurt something awful.
And if you didn't run fast enough, they went after you.
And shootings were every day's chance.
Just to give you an example, there was a time when I had a chance to get some food from the outside-- I'll come to that later-- and I was caught.
And I was-- there was a deep snow at the time, and I was stood up there, and a guy took his rifle and shot me.
And I don't know whether he did it on purpose on not.
I was glanced in my left temple, and I remember absolutely nothing.
I only remember that I fell-- I was told I fell into the snow, and I was in a-- in a puddle of blood.
And rumor went, Nash was killed today, which was nothing unusual because it happened all the time.
There was not a day where not one or two of us were just killed.
But my friends dragged me back into the barracks, and somehow I-- I survived, and I came to, and I was in the labor-- in the work detail the next morning because you couldn't stay home.
If you would stay home, you was shipped out.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was impossible to survive on the rations that you get-- you got, and that was the purpose of it.
This whole system was designed that you should work as much as you had in you, but the end result was death.
It was slave labor.
We were slaves.
That means we had absolutely no law or recall of any kind in our favor.
We were stripped of any human dignity you can imagine.
We were exposed to the most sadistic, incredible indignities.
We were beaten.
We were pushed.
We were tortured.
And of course enemy number one was this incredible hunger, which was with us 24 hours a day.
And with it came the depression and the despondency, and a lot of people just couldn't take it, and they gave up.
And you could see if one became marked that he's not going to be around any longer.
He became very pale, and he was swelling up.
He had a swollen face, he had swollen legs.
He became unbelievably fatigued, and we had trouble keeping him up at the appel and on the march to the place.
And whenever a man became what was-- what we called a muselman, a muselman was the one marked for death to come soon.
Invariably, it was only a few days, and they were dead.
And not only we knew it, but they knew it.
Whoever was a muselman knew this is his end.
And they took their arrangements for their death.
I want to mention that the reason I'm here was that we were three of us who stuck together through thick and thin and helped each other as much as we possibly could.
And I can say without exaggeration, all three of us survived because of our bond that we had.
The two were Pavel Loiv, who then, after the war, went  to Australia, and unfortunately died two-- two years ago.
The third one was my dear friend Sammy Schmueli, who is now in a kibbutz in Israel, and I just yesterday received a lovely letter from him.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] One of our muselmen was Dr.
Solomon, the man who saved his boots.
And he told me, when I go, be sure to get my shoes, because in my left heel is a piece of gold in the heel.
So if you get into a situation where you need to bribe somebody, you will have something.
So the three of us had-- the-- the-- the reason I was chosen to wear the boots, because I had very small feet, and they fit me.
The others had big feet.
So I was wearing these shoes.
And much later, when things became very, very bad, and we were cut off from our outside world and our supplier-- which will-- I explain later-- we decided we have to open our heel.
What will we do with the gold?
We knew our way around.
There were people who we knew could be bribed, and there was particularly one guy in the Waffen SS.
Which, Waffen SS was not a real SS.
It was kind of a army unit, only it was attached to the SS.
And this guy was known to be open to bribes.
And we labored one night to open this heel.
Now, I don't know whether you ever tried to remove a heel with a makeshift kind of a knife that was not a real knife.
It was a very hard piece of labor, and sometimes we really thought we wouldn't be able to do it.
But finally, we were able to open this heel.
And lo and behold, there was an opening, but there was nothing in it.
So we were again where we started out.
Dr.
Solomon died with the opinion, or with the idea he had an emergency exit in his heel.
But he didn't, because the guy who was supposed to put the gold in kept it for himself.
He probably knew this Jew is never-- no-- never come back, so what does he need this gold for.
And he kept it for himself.
But another friend of ours, Mr.
Wertheimer from Munich, he became a muselman.
And he told me, when I go, I have a lower bridge in my mouth which is pure gold.
Would you please take it out and make use of it?
And he died, and we did.
And we took the gold, and we approached the SS man, and the SS man took it gladly.
And what did we buy with it?
We bought the only thing that could keep us alive.
That is, a place in the kitchen service.

To be in the kitchen was like a life saver, because although you couldn't get any delicacies or-- or pheasant under glass, but you had access to a little more soup and to a little more potato peels, which would be otherwise thrown out, or other treasures which could keep us alive.
And I was put into the-- we-- we drew lots, and I was chosen to go into the kitchen.
And in this short time I was in the kitchen, we really revived because I could get some of my strength back.
And of course I could feed my two friends, which were just as essential as myself.
And it bought us another few days.
Every day that we survived was a miracle.
Every day that we chalked up as survival was a success.
And so we had this reprieve.
And one day, our protector-- our guy in the Waffen SS-- obviously was found out.
Anyway, he disappeared.
He was put, probably, onto the Russian front.
And in this moment, the kapo of the kitchen who knew I was just put in by this guy, and he has other friends to put in there, I was given the most horrible beating I ever had in my life.
I don't know whoever received a beating, but this beating hurt unbearably so.
And that was the end of my kitchen adventure.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Our survival was dependent on a variety of miracles, or a chain of events, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] happenstances, luck, or whatever you want to call it.
One day, we were on a work outfit, and there was a-- a yell, is there a doctor around?
Is there a doctor around?
Well, that was very suspicious because they use these tricks usually.
Is there somebody who knows to type?
Or is there somebody who knows how to write?
Or is there somebody who knows languages?
And whenever somebody volunteered, they were given carrying corpses or cleaning latrines, or some kind of other occupation.
But I thought, well, I'll raise my hand.
And lo and behold, they took me to the road, and there was a child who had fallen off a car-- one of these wagons-- and he fainted.
He had a concussion.
And I brought him to.
Believe it or not, I didn't do very much to him.
I saw that he would come to anyway.
But in the eyes of the father, I saved this child's life.
And from this moment on, he, endangering his own freedom and life, supplied us with some additional food.
That was a German peasant, and he used to--
Pardon?
I don't remember his name, but I know where he was.
He was in the village of Landsberg there.
And this guy gave us a beet here, or a potato there, or he gave us these little shallots with little onions.
It was, in the true sense of the word, life savers.
Because as I said, the ration alone was just here to-- to starve you out.
Unfortunately, this source was cut off one day because we had a case of typhus, and we were quarantined.
They didn't care whether we died of typhus or of something else, but they were deadly afraid that this is an epidemic, and they will get it.
So we were quarantined, and that happened on the 20th of April of 1944.
Of course.
Of course.
All these days, this innate optimism stayed with us.
We had all news items.
We knew about the landings in Normandy.
We knew about the miserable state of the German military at that time.
We knew that it was the last moment.
The-- the irony is, when my family was put in a gas chamber in October, it was only seven months before the war was over.
And the war was over seven months later because of Mr. de Gaulle.
If Mr. de Gaulle hadn't entered the war, the war would have been over not in May, but in February.
But de Gaulle had to insist that Paris had to be liberated first so the German could catch their breath.
There was the Battle of the Bulge.
So it-- but there was postponement of the peace.
But all our-- the-- the-- the bulk of our losses, of our murdered people, was in this seven months, when the Germans were losing the war.
